title: Anomaly Detection Evaluation Challenges
concept: Evaluating anomaly detectors is uniquely hard because true anomalies are rare by definition, making standard accuracy metrics misleading and requiring careful attention to precision, recall, and threshold tuning.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What tradeoff occurs when a manufacturing anomaly detection threshold is set extremely tight?

options.text: It reduces missed defects, but produces more false positives on normal parts, slowing the line
It has no effect on false positives or false negatives at all

exercises.explain: Yes! Tighter thresholds trade fewer misses for more false alarms.

cards.title: The needle-in-a-haystack evaluation problem
cards.body: If defects occur in only 1 out of every 10,000 parts, a lazy detector that NEVER flags anything achieves 99.99% raw accuracy — while being completely useless. This is the same class-imbalance trap from Unit 128, but even more extreme in anomaly detection.

cards.title: Evaluating Anomaly Detectors
cards.body: Because true anomalies are rare by definition, evaluation relies heavily on precision (of flagged items, how many are truly anomalous), recall (of all true anomalies, how many were caught), and the tradeoff curve between them as the detection threshold changes — raw accuracy is nearly meaningless here.

cards.title: Fishing with the right net size
cards.body: A net with tiny holes (very sensitive threshold) catches every fish but also scoops up tons of seaweed and debris (low precision). A net with huge holes (loose threshold) only catches debris-free water and misses most fish too (low recall). Evaluation is about finding the net size that balances both.

cards.title: Pharmaceutical defect evaluation
cards.body: A pill inspection system evaluated on 100,000 pills, of which only 20 are truly defective. Reporting '99.98% accuracy' by flagging nothing is meaningless — the real evaluation question is: of the pills flagged as anomalous, how many were truly defective (precision), and of the 20 true defects, how many did the system catch (recall)?

cards.title: There is no single 'correct' threshold
cards.body: It's a myth that there's one universally correct anomaly threshold. The right threshold depends entirely on the cost of a missed defect (a recalled product, a safety incident) versus the cost of a false alarm (wasted inspection time, discarded good parts) — this is a business decision informed by the precision-recall tradeoff, not a fixed technical answer.

cards.title: Why Raw Accuracy Fails Here
cards.body: The class imbalance trap in anomaly evaluation:

left.text:
Misleading with rare anomalies
A 'flag nothing' model scores near 100%
Hides total failure to detect defects

right.text:
Reveals true detection performance
Shows tradeoffs at different thresholds
Standard for imbalanced problems

exercises.prompt: Why is raw accuracy a poor metric for evaluating anomaly detection systems?

options.text: Because true anomalies are rare, a model that flags nothing can still score very high accuracy while being useless
Because accuracy cannot be computed for image-based tasks at all
Because anomaly detectors never produce any measurable output
Because precision and recall are always identical to accuracy

exercises.explain: Correct! Extreme class imbalance makes raw accuracy misleading for rare-anomaly problems.

exercises.prompt: True or false: There is one universally correct anomaly detection threshold that works for every application.
exercises.explain: False. The right threshold depends on the specific cost of missed defects versus false alarms for that application.

exercises.sentence: Because anomalies are rare, evaluation relies heavily on precision and ___ rather than raw accuracy.
exercises.explain: Precision and recall reveal true detection performance despite class imbalance.

exercises.prompt: Why does reporting '99.98% accuracy' for a pill defect detector that flags nothing tell you almost nothing useful?

options.text: Because with only 20 defective pills out of 100,000, flagging nothing still achieves near-perfect accuracy while catching zero real defects
Because accuracy is impossible to calculate for pill images
Because 99.98% is always considered a failing score regardless of context

exercises.explain: Correct! Extreme rarity of true positives makes raw accuracy hide total detection failure.

exercises.prompt: We have completed our study of Anomaly & Defect Detection — one-class learning, reconstruction-based methods, manufacturing deployment, and evaluation challenges. What is the next logical computer vision application to study?

options.text: Depth Estimation — recovering 3D distance information from 2D images
Restarting the curriculum from basic image filters
Deleting the anomaly detection pipeline entirely
Skipping directly to deployment without further vision topics

exercises.explain: Exactly! Now let's move from 'is this normal?' to a new question: 'how far away is this?' Next up: Depth Estimation!
